title: The Role of Intuition in Health Decisions
meta_description: Trust your gut. It already knows what your body needs. This article shows you how to stop second guessing and start listening.
og_title: The Role of Intuition in Health Decisions
og_description: Trust your gut. It already knows what your body needs. This article shows you how to stop second guessing and start listening.
body:
You Already Know What Your Body Needs (You Just Don't Trust It Yet)

Let me tell you something that might make you uncomfortable. You've probably made a health decision in the last week that your gut told you was wrong. Maybe you ate something that didn't sit right, pushed through a workout when your body was screaming for rest, or ignored that quiet whisper telling you to call a doctor. And you did it anyway. Why? Because you've been trained your whole life to trust the external over the internal. The test results, the doctor's opinion, the "expert" advice, the calorie count, the step tracker. You've learned to doubt the one source that actually knows you best: your own intuition.

I'm Kalesh, and I work with people who feel things deeply. The ones who walk into a room and know the mood before anyone speaks. The ones who get a physical sensation when something's off with their body or someone else's. The ones who've been called "too sensitive," "dramatic," or "anxious" their whole lives. Here's the truth: nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most people's. And when it comes to your health, that "too sensitive" system is actually your greatest asset. You just need to learn how to use it without second-guessing yourself into a corner.

So let's talk about the role of intuition in health decisions. Not the woo-woo, "ask the universe what to eat for breakfast" version. The real, grounded, practical way your body talks to you every single day. And how to stop overriding it with other people's opinions.

"Your body doesn't lie to you. It can't. It doesn't have the capacity for deception. Your mind does that all by itself when it tries to talk you out of what you already know."

Why Your Intuition Is Actually a Biological System, Not a Magic Trick

Here's something most people don't get: intuition isn't some mystical gift reserved for psychics and gurus. It's a biological feedback system that your body has been running since the day you were born. Your gut has more neurons than your spinal cord. Your heart has its own neural network. Your body is literally wired to sense things before your conscious mind catches up. That's not magic. That's physiology.

When you get a "bad feeling" about a medication, a treatment, or even a certain food, what's actually happening is your body is processing information your brain hasn't consciously registered yet. Maybe it's a subtle shift in your blood chemistry. Maybe it's a micro-expression on your doctor's face that you caught subconsciously. Maybe it's the way a supplement made you feel slightly off three days ago, and your body remembers even though your mind forgot. Right?! Your body keeps the score, whether you're paying attention or not.

The problem isn't that you don't have intuition. The problem is that you've been taught to override it. Every time you felt something was wrong and someone told you "you're overthinking it" or "the test results say you're fine," you learned to silence your own internal guidance. And now you're walking around with a perfectly good navigation system that you've been trained to ignore.

The Three Ways Your Body Talks to You About Health

Your intuition doesn't speak in complete sentences. It doesn't say, "Hey, you should probably avoid dairy for the next three weeks because your gut lining is inflamed." It talks in sensations, impulses, and feelings. Here are the three main channels, and you probably use all of them without realizing it.

1. Physical Sensations (The Gut Feeling)

This is the most obvious one. You feel something in your body before you think it. A tightness in your chest when you consider a certain treatment. A sinking feeling in your stomach when you read the side effects of a medication. A sudden relaxation when someone suggests something that's actually right for you. These aren't random. They're your body's way of saying yes or no before your brain gets involved.

I had a client once who kept getting a sharp pain in her left shoulder every time she thought about going to a particular specialist. She ignored it for months because the specialist came highly recommended. Turns out, the guy had a history of dismissing women's pain. Her body knew before she did. She just hadn't learned to listen yet.

2. Emotional Signals (The "Off" Feeling)

This one is trickier because emotions are easy to dismiss as "just anxiety." But here's the thing: anxiety and intuition feel different in the body. Anxiety is frantic, looping, and repetitive. It's the same thought playing on repeat like a broken record. Intuition is calm, clear, and usually a single knowing. It doesn't argue with you. It just states the fact and sits there.

If you feel uneasy about a health decision and that unease keeps coming back to the same point without spiraling into other worries, that's not anxiety. That's intuition. Pay attention to it.

3. Sudden Knowing (The Download)

This is the one that freaks people out the most. You're just going about your day, and suddenly you know something. Maybe it's "I need to get a second opinion on this diagnosis." Maybe it's "That supplement is not right for me." Maybe it's "I need to stop eating this food group." The knowing comes out of nowhere, fully formed, with no logical trail behind it. That's your intuition bypassing your rational mind entirely.

Does that land? Because this is where most people get stuck. They think, "But I don't have a reason for knowing this. How can I trust it?" Here's the answer: you don't need a reason. Your body has processed information you don't have conscious access to. Trust the knowing, even if you can't explain it yet.

Why Western Medicine Often Misses This (And Why You Need Both)

I'm not here to tell you to throw out your doctor's advice and live on vibes alone. That would be stupid and dangerous. Western medicine is incredible at certain things: acute emergencies, infections, broken bones, surgeries. It saves lives every single day. But here's what it's not good at: treating the whole person, accounting for individual variation, and honoring the subtle signals your body sends.

Your doctor has data. They have studies, statistics, and protocols. But they don't have your data. They don't know how a medication feels in your body. They don't know that you always get a headache after eating a certain food, even though the allergy test came back negative. They don't know that you feel worse after a particular treatment, even though the numbers look good on paper.

That's where your intuition comes in. It's the missing piece. It's the data your doctor can't access. And when you combine medical expertise with your own internal knowing, you get something powerful: informed, personalized health decisions that actually work for you.

If you want to dive deeper into how your body holds onto experiences and memories that shape your intuition, I highly recommend checking out The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's a dense read, but it'll change how you understand the connection between your physical body and your inner knowing. See on Amazon (paid link)

How to Start Trusting Your Intuition About Health (Practical Steps)

You don't need to become a psychic to use your intuition in health decisions. You just need to start paying attention and stop overriding yourself. Here's how to do that without losing your mind.

Step 1: Create a Body Journal

Get a notebook. Every day, write down three things: what you ate, how you felt physically, and any "hunches" you had about your health. Don't judge them. Don't explain them. Just write them down. Over time, you'll start to see patterns. "Every time I eat gluten, I feel foggy the next day, even though my test says I'm not sensitive." "Every time I consider going to that acupuncturist, I feel a sense of calm." The patterns are your intuition speaking in a language you can finally understand.

Step 2: Practice the Pause

Before you make any health decision - taking a supplement, trying a new diet, agreeing to a procedure - pause for 10 seconds. Take a breath. Ask your body: "Is this right for me?" Notice what happens. Do you feel a sense of expansion or contraction? Openness or resistance? Don't overthink it. Just notice. The first answer is usually the right one. Your mind will jump in immediately after with doubts. That's fine. Just notice the initial response before the mind takes over.

Step 3: Start Small

Don't try to trust your intuition on something huge like cancer treatment right away. Start with small, low-stakes decisions. What should I eat for lunch? Should I take a walk or rest? Do I need to drink more water? Practice on the small stuff, and you'll build trust for the big stuff. It's like building a muscle. You don't start with the heaviest weight. You start with what you can handle and work your way up.

Step 4: Get Quiet

Your intuition is always talking, but if you're constantly scrolling, working, or distracting yourself, you won't hear it. Carve out 5 minutes a day of total silence. No phone. No music. No podcast. Just you and your breath. Your body will start sending you messages you've been missing because you've been too busy to listen.

What To Do When Your Intuition Contradicts Your Doctor

This is the hard one. Your doctor says one thing. Your gut says another. What do you do? First, don't panic. This doesn't mean you have to ignore your doctor. It means you need to investigate further.

Ask your doctor more questions. "Why do you recommend this? What are the alternatives? What happens if we wait?" Pay attention to how you feel when they answer. Does their explanation feel complete and honest, or does it feel like they're brushing you off? Your intuition can pick up on things like a doctor's tone, body language, and willingness to engage with your concerns.

If something still feels off, get a second opinion. Not because your doctor is wrong, but because your intuition is telling you there's more information you need. And here's the key: you don't have to choose between your intuition and your doctor. You can use your intuition to inform your conversation with your doctor. "I have a feeling this might not be the right approach for me. Can we explore other options?" A good doctor will respect that. A bad one will dismiss it. That's useful information too.

For a more accessible read on how to tune into your body's signals, check out How to Do the Work by Dr. Nicole LePera. She breaks down the mind-body connection in a way that's practical without being overwhelming. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Problem With "Just Trust Your Gut" (And What To Do Instead)

I know I've been telling you to trust your intuition, but I need to be honest with you about something. "Just trust your gut" is terrible advice if you don't know the difference between intuition and fear. Because here's the thing: fear can feel a lot like intuition, especially when you're new to this. Fear is loud, urgent, and panicky. Intuition is quiet, steady, and certain. Fear says "What if something goes wrong?" Intuition says "Something is off, and here's what it is."

So how do you tell the difference? You practice. You start with small decisions where the stakes are low. You notice the difference between the frantic energy of fear and the calm knowing of intuition. Over time, you get better at distinguishing them. And you also learn that intuition doesn't always tell you what you want to hear. Sometimes it tells you to do the hard thing. Sometimes it tells you to make a change you don't want to make. That doesn't mean it's wrong. It means it's honest.

If you're struggling to tell the difference between intuition and anxiety, there's a great book called Playing Big by Tara Mohr that covers this exact topic. She calls it the "Inner Critic vs. Inner Mentor" distinction, and it's a breakthrough. See on Amazon (paid link)

When Your Intuition Tells You Something Nobody Else Believes

This is where it gets lonely. You have a knowing about your health that doesn't match what anyone else is saying. Your family thinks you're being dramatic. Your doctor thinks you're anxious. Your friends tell you to stop Googling symptoms. And you're sitting there, feeling something real, with no one to validate it.

I see this all the time in my work. People who knew something was wrong with their body months or years before it showed up on any test. People who changed their diet because of a "weird feeling" and later discovered they had a sensitivity that no test could have caught. People who avoided a treatment that "everyone else" said was fine, only to find out later it had side effects that were being downplayed.

You are not crazy. You are not dramatic. You are not making things up. You are picking up on information that others can't perceive because their nervous systems aren't calibrated the same way. That's not a weakness. That's a gift. But it's a gift that comes with a price: you have to learn to trust yourself even when no one else does.

The Bottom Line

Your intuition is not a replacement for medical care. It's a complement. It's the missing piece that makes health decisions truly personalized. Your doctor has the external data. You have the internal data. When you put them together, you get the full picture.

Start small. Practice the pause. Keep a body journal. Get quiet. And most of all, stop apologizing for being sensitive. Your sensitivity is not a flaw. It's your internal guidance system, and it's been trying to help you all along. You just had to learn how to listen.

If you're ready to go deeper into trusting your intuition around health and other life decisions, I've put together a free assessment that can help you identify where you're overriding your intuition and how to start trusting it more. You can take it at this link or check out the quick quiz to see where you're at right now.
